items.correct: Whether human behaviour is shaped more by social structures or individual choice.
items.misconception: Whether buildings or workers are more important to society.
items.why: Students take 'structure' to mean physical structures. In sociology, structure = enduring patterns/institutions; agency = individual capacity to act. Giddens proposes both interact (structuration).

items.q: Why do positivists prefer quantitative methods?
items.correct: They produce objective, reliable, generalisable data resembling natural science.
items.misconception: Because numbers are easier to collect than words.
items.why: Students reduce the choice to convenience. Positivism is a philosophical commitment to studying society scientifically - the preference is about EPISTEMOLOGY, not ease.

items.q: What do interpretivists seek when researching society?
items.why: Students apply positivist goals to interpretivism. Interpretivists (Weber) reject the natural-science model - humans interpret and give meaning, so research must access meaning, not measure behaviour.
It's Giddens. “Bourdieu” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Bourdieu's term for internalised dispositions producing practice is what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Habitus. “Field” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Giddens publish 'The Constitution of Society'?
items.why: The real figure is 1984. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
